name: Policy-Procurement Alignment Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A procurement clause mandating platform neutrality is drafted and submitted to the Digitaliseringsstyrelsen. Systemic: The clause reduces vendor lock‑in risk by 30% as measured by the dependency index over the next 24 months, lowering the probability of single‑vendor outages. Strategic: While this enhances national resilience, it may slow procurement cycles, forcing a trade‑off between speed of deployment and sovereignty of the digital identity ecosystem.
options: Draft a minimal compliance clause that references existing eIDAS standards, ensuring no new vendor requirements.
Introduce a phased procurement requirement that mandates platform neutrality for future releases, with a 12‑month compliance window.
Propose a market‑based incentive model that rewards suppliers offering platform‑neutral fallback paths, leveraging a subscription‑based certification program.
review: Controls speed vs. sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider budget constraints.

name: Technical Demonstrator Development Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A FIDO2/WebAuthn demonstrator is built on GrapheneOS and tested with a simulated identity‑provider environment. Systemic: The demonstrator increases stakeholder confidence by 25% as measured by demo sign‑ups and feedback surveys, proving that fallback authentication is feasible without Apple or Google. Strategic: This technical validation strengthens the case for platform neutrality but may divert limited resources from policy advocacy, creating a trade‑off between technical depth and stakeholder engagement.
options: Develop a lightweight mobile demo using open‑source libraries on GrapheneOS, focusing on core authentication flows.
Expand to a cross‑platform browser demo that integrates hardware‑token fallback, covering both mobile and desktop use cases.
Deploy a cloud‑based micro‑service that simulates the entire identity stack, enabling rapid testing of fallback mechanisms and generating real‑time analytics.
review: Controls technical depth vs stakeholder engagement. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory approval timelines.

name: Coalition Building & Advocacy Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Commitments from three civil‑society groups are secured to co‑author a joint statement on platform neutrality. Systemic: Public pressure rises by 40% as measured by petition signatures and media mentions, amplifying the political signal to procurement bodies. Strategic: The broadened coalition builds momentum but risks diluting focus, balancing breadth of influence against depth of policy impact.
options: Engage a small, focused coalition of privacy NGOs to draft a joint statement.
Expand outreach to include academia, security firms, and consumer groups, creating a multi‑stakeholder task force.
Launch a public campaign using social media influencers and hackathon events to create a nationwide movement for platform neutrality.
review: Controls breadth vs depth. Weakness: The options fail to consider legal liability of coalition members.

name: Standards & EU Engagement Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A technical brief on fallback requirements is submitted to the EU Digital Identity Wallet working group. Systemic: The brief contributes to a 15% increase in EU policy references to platform neutrality, as tracked in WG minutes, aligning Danish goals with European standards. Strategic: This alignment accelerates harmonization but may delay national implementation, creating a trade‑off between alignment with EU policy and autonomy in domestic procurement.
options: Submit a concise technical note highlighting fallback requirements to the EU WG.
Co‑author a white paper with EU partners to shape the next draft of the Digital Identity Wallet specification.
Establish a joint EU‑Danish working group that pilots a platform‑neutral wallet prototype using blockchain‑based identity tokens.
review: Controls alignment vs autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross‑border data protection constraints.

name: Risk & Resilience Framing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A risk assessment report frames platform dependence as a national security threat and is published to government stakeholders. Systemic: The report prompts a 20% rise in risk‑management budget allocations, as reflected in government spending reports, underscoring the urgency of fallback solutions. Strategic: Elevating the risk narrative increases urgency but may trigger political backlash, balancing risk framing against political feasibility.
options: Release a standard risk report using existing threat‑matrix templates.
Produce a scenario‑based analysis that quantifies potential downtime costs under platform outages.
Develop an interactive simulation platform that allows policymakers to visualize cascading failures, powered by AI‑driven predictive analytics.
review: Controls risk framing vs political feasibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider stakeholder fatigue.

name: Digital Identity Resilience Architecture Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A resilience architecture blueprint is drafted, outlining fallback paths. Systemic: The blueprint reduces single‑vendor failure probability by 35% as measured by simulation risk index. Strategic: While this enhances system resilience, it may increase upfront design complexity, trading speed of deployment for long‑term stability.
options: Draft a resilience architecture that reuses existing eIDAS components with minimal changes.
Extend the architecture to include a dedicated fallback authentication service that can be activated during platform outages.
Build a decentralized identity layer using blockchain‑based verifiable credentials that operates independently of any mobile OS.
review: Controls resilience vs. speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider budget constraints.

name: Platform‑Neutral Access Pathway Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A platform‑neutral access protocol is defined using open standards. Systemic: Adoption of the protocol is projected to increase platform‑neutral authentication adoption by 20% within the next 18 months, measured by pilot deployments. Strategic: This reduces dependency on Apple/Google but may require additional developer effort, trading ease of integration for broader neutrality.
options: Specify a WebAuthn‑only flow that can be integrated into existing mobile apps.
Add a browser‑based fallback that uses QR codes to bridge to mobile authentication.
Introduce a cross‑platform biometric token that leverages edge‑AI for on‑device authentication, eliminating OS reliance.
review: Controls platform neutrality vs developer effort. Weakness: The options fail to consider user experience variability.

name: Stakeholder Engagement & Procurement Leverage Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A stakeholder engagement plan is launched to secure commitments from key ministries. Systemic: The plan is expected to secure at least 5 formal endorsements, raising procurement influence by 30% as measured by signed memoranda. Strategic: While increasing influence, the plan may consume significant time, trading rapid policy action for broader stakeholder buy‑in.
options: Conduct a series of targeted briefings with procurement officials.
Organize a joint workshop with ministries, academia, and industry to co‑create procurement language.
Establish a public‑private partnership that co‑finances a platform‑neutral procurement framework, creating a shared ownership model.
review: Controls stakeholder buy‑in vs. speed of action. Weakness: The options fail to consider political risk of partnership.

name: Regulatory Compliance & Certification Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A compliance roadmap is drafted to align fallback mechanisms with eIDAS and NSIS. Systemic: The roadmap is projected to reduce certification time by 25% for future identity solutions, measured by audit cycle duration. Strategic: This accelerates market entry but may increase regulatory scrutiny, trading speed for heightened oversight.
options: Map existing fallback designs against current eIDAS requirements.
Propose a modular certification pathway that allows incremental compliance checks.
Develop a self‑attestation framework powered by zero‑knowledge proofs that bypasses traditional certification bottlenecks.
review: Controls certification speed vs regulatory scrutiny. Weakness: The options fail to consider legal enforceability of self‑attestation.

name: Innovation & Emerging Tech Adoption Strategy
consequences: Immediate: An innovation roadmap is created to evaluate emerging authentication technologies. Systemic: The roadmap identifies at least three viable technologies that could reduce platform lock‑in by 40%, measured by technology readiness level assessments. Strategic: While fostering innovation, the roadmap may divert resources from core deliverables, trading breadth of exploration for depth of execution.
options: Evaluate FIDO2 extensions for enhanced security.
Pilot a hardware‑token ecosystem that supports cross‑platform authentication.
Deploy a quantum‑resistant cryptographic protocol that operates on low‑power edge devices, ensuring future‑proof platform neutrality.
review: Controls innovation breadth vs resource allocation. Weakness: The options fail to consider maturity of quantum‑resistant algorithms.

name: Digital Identity Supply Chain Diversification Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Identify alternative vendor ecosystems for identity components. Systemic: Diversification reduces vendor concentration risk by 25% as measured by the dependency index. Strategic: This increases resilience but may increase integration complexity, trading simplicity for robustness.
options: Map current vendor footprint and propose incremental diversification within existing procurement cycles.
Introduce a dual‑vendor strategy that simultaneously supports Apple/Google and an open‑source alternative.
Deploy a fully open‑source identity stack on a community‑maintained platform, eliminating commercial vendor reliance.
review: Controls vendor simplicity vs resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider cost escalation of open‑source maintenance.

name: Public‑Sector Procurement Incentive Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Design incentive mechanisms for suppliers to adopt platform‑neutral solutions. Systemic: Incentives increase platform‑neutral supplier participation by 30% as measured by bid submissions. Strategic: This encourages innovation but may raise procurement costs, trading cost efficiency for supplier diversity.
options: Offer a small performance bonus for compliance with existing neutrality clauses.
Implement a tiered subsidy program that rewards incremental platform‑neutral features.
Create a revenue‑sharing model where suppliers receive a share of savings from reduced vendor lock‑in.
review: Controls cost efficiency vs supplier diversity. Weakness: The options fail to consider legal constraints on subsidy structures.

name: Citizen‑Centric Adoption Pathway Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Develop user‑friendly fallback authentication flows. Systemic: Adoption of fallback flows increases user confidence by 20% as measured by survey metrics. Strategic: Enhancing usability may require additional user education, trading immediate simplicity for long‑term adoption.
options: Provide a simple QR‑code fallback for existing users.
Integrate a biometric‑based fallback that works across devices.
Deploy a decentralized identity wallet that users control, enabling zero‑trust authentication without platform involvement.
review: Controls user simplicity vs long‑term adoption. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy implications of biometric fallback.

name: Governance & Oversight Enhancement Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Establish a governance board to oversee fallback implementation. Systemic: Board oversight reduces policy drift by 15% as measured by audit findings. Strategic: Strong governance increases accountability but may slow decision‑making, trading agility for oversight.
options: Form a standing committee within existing procurement bodies.
Create a cross‑agency oversight council with defined reporting metrics.
Set up an independent regulatory authority empowered to enforce platform‑neutral standards.
review: Controls agility vs accountability. Weakness: The options fail to consider resource allocation for the new authority.

name: Economic Impact & Funding Leverage Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Quantify economic benefits of reduced platform lock‑in. Systemic: Economic analysis projects a 10% increase in national digital economy output over five years, measured by GDP contribution. Strategic: Highlighting economic gains may secure funding but could divert focus from technical deliverables, trading financial advocacy for technical depth.
options: Produce a cost‑benefit report using existing data.
Conduct a scenario analysis that includes indirect savings from reduced vendor fees.
Launch a public‑private investment fund that channels capital into platform‑neutral identity startups.
review: Controls financial advocacy vs technical depth. Weakness: The options fail to consider investor appetite for early‑stage identity projects.
